review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for reinforcement questions on the exact algebraic basis and mechanics of data parallelism. The reasoning is stated verbally, so a separate clip is unnecessary.

question_bank.question: Why does averaging gradients across M GPUs in data parallelism give the same result as using one giant device on the full macrobatch?
choices: Because gradients are linear with respect to the averaged minibatch loss
Because each GPU uses a different optimizer
Because all-reduce changes the objective function
Because duplicated examples cancel each other out
question_bank.explanation: When the loss is an average over examples, the gradient of that average equals the average of the per-example or per-shard gradients, so the result matches exactly.

transcript_quotes: And if you think about mathematically
So in practice, if you're computing a single scalar

question_bank.question: What is a correctness bug in data-parallel training?
choices: Giving each GPU a different minibatch shard
Accidentally loading the same minibatch on every GPU
Keeping identical model weights on each GPU at the start
question_bank.explanation: Data parallelism relies on each GPU processing different samples so the global batch is larger. Duplicating the same minibatch wastes compute and breaks the intended scaling.

transcript_quotes: need to load different minibatches of data.
code, where they actually accidentally load

question_bank.question: After each GPU computes its local gradients in data parallelism, what happens next?
choices: Each GPU updates independently using only its own gradients
Gradients are all-reduced so every GPU can make the same update
Weights are sharded to one owner GPU per layer
Activations are discarded and recomputed before the update
question_bank.explanation: All-reduce gives every device the same averaged gradient tensor, so identical local copies of the model remain synchronized after the update.

transcript_quotes: So this is where we do an all reduce operation.
